mnemonics.title: IF-ID-EX-MEM-WB Pipeline
mnemonics.content: I Find It Extremely Memorable While Buying - Remember the 5 stages: Instruction Fetch, Instruction Decode, Execute, Memory Access, Write Back

mnemonics.title: Interrupt Handling Steps
mnemonics.content: DISE - Detect Interrupt, Identify Source, Save State, Execute ISR, Restore State - DISE handles interrupts with ease!

mnemonics.title: Pipeline Flush on Interrupt
mnemonics.content: When interrupt arrives, pipeline gets 'drained' like water from a pipe - flush all stages, start fresh from ISR

mnemonics.title: Types of Interrupts
mnemonics.content: TIN - Trappable (software), Interrupt (hardware), Non-maskable (critical) - TIN helps sort them out

acronyms.usage: The specific code executed when an interrupt occurs; like answering the phone when it rings
A table storing addresses of ISRs; use when you need to find which ISR handles which interrupt
Hardware that manages interrupt requests; remember when multiple devices compete for CPU attention
Critical interrupts that cannot be ignored (like power failure); use for emergencies that must be handled
The hardware line used to signal an interrupt; think of it as the 'doorbell' for the CPU

visualTips.visualization: Imagine a car factory assembly line where different workers do different tasks on each car. Each instruction is like a car moving through stations. When interrupt hits, it's like a customer demanding a custom order - you must stop the line, finish their order, then resume normal production.

Think of CPU working on a important document (main program). Interrupt is like a phone ringing - you must save your place (save state), answer (execute ISR), then return to your document (restore state and continue).
Visualize a water pipeline. When interrupt occurs, you 'drain' the water from all stages - instructions in progress are discarded, pipeline empties, then fresh water (ISR) starts flowing.
Picture a queue at a ticket counter. Latency is the waiting time from raising hand (interrupt request) to getting served (ISR execution). Faster response = smaller queue.

analogies.analogy: A laundry washing machine with multiple stages: wash, dry, fold. While one load is drying, you wash the next. This overlapping saves time just like instruction pipelining.

You are typing an essay (main program) and your phone rings (interrupt). You put a bookmark where you stopped (save state), answer the phone (execute ISR - take a message), then return to your essay from where you left off (restore state).

You're watching a movie (main program) and someone knocks at the door (interrupt 1). While answering, your smoke detector goes off (interrupt 2, higher priority). You handle the emergency first, then return to the door, then back to the movie.
In a hospital emergency room, a critical patient (NMI) gets treated before someone with a broken arm (lower priority interrupt). The triage nurse (PIC) decides who gets CPU attention first.
